body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Applicability of TVR YANG Data Models
Using off-path mechanisms for exposing Time-Variant Routing information
2 - Using Off-Path Mechanisms For Exposing Time-Variant Routing Information

transcript.text:
SPEAKER S1: 4:00. Alright. Uh, why don't we get started? It is 4:00. Uh, welcome to iATf 124. Uh, TVR working Group. Ah. And we. And this works now. All right. A couple of things. I, I know this is the middle of the week, uh, but for those of you who may be attending who are new a note. Well, and a reminder of our iATf policies, I will simply leave this up for, for just a second, calling, uh, particular attention to codes of conduct and anti-harassment policy. And again, a second focus on conduct. Uh, as we continue to have technical discussions, uh, we are hard on ideas, but easy on people. Okay. Uh, a couple other things. If you are here in the room, uh, please make sure that you are joining the session, uh, via data tracker, uh, and using the on site tool. Uh, there's also a mobile version. And, um, if you are looking for where the agenda is, uh, or other information specific, uh, the links are here, but you probably already know that because you are here. Alright. Uh, continuing on, uh, we we do need to have, uh, minute takers. Uh, I, I know that, uh, in the past, we've had, uh, Adam has, uh, volunteered for that. And the more minute takers we have, the better. So, uh, please make sure to get into the shared notepad and help us out. All right. Uh, for this particular working group, I know that we have a light agenda. Uh, we have made very good progress on our initial chartered items. We do have several chartered items that are, uh, wrapping up and waiting for reviews and write ups. Uh, right now, uh, if we look at our milestones, we had the initial use cases document, which was done and published as RFC 9657. Uh, our requirements document is ready for ESG review pending a Shepherd's write up, which is on me. Uh, we do have a data model, which is, uh, we'll be talking about today in an applicability statement. Additionally, in the original charter, we had some work related to implementation and operational considerations. Uh, we have not begun work on that particular, uh, charter item yet. Waiting to finish. Requirements and data model and applicability as requirements and data model are finishing up as applicability is well underway. Uh, a question here, either to those online or those in the room. Uh, do we still believe as a working group that there is utility in, uh, documenting implementation and operational considerations for those attempting to use the data model or use the applicability statement? Now, I will before going forward, let me pause there. If you have an opinion on that, please take a moment and join the queue. Rick.
SPEAKER S2: Rick. Taylor. What do you mean by use? Because I think there's two different meanings. If I can propose. What I think you mean by use is either. Do I understand how to integrate these Yang extensions into existing Yang models to, to to carry some of the, the timing information, scheduling information? Or do you mean what I should do as a routing protocol if this information is provided to me? One is a much bigger problem than the other.
SPEAKER S1: Did you.
SPEAKER S3: Um, so the stuff that is what the routing protocol should do is supposed to go into the applicability statement. Um, and so the implementation and operational considerations really should be about I've implemented the code and here are the potholes I found.
SPEAKER S1: Are you?
SPEAKER S4: From future. Wait. I think one thing for operation we need to consider is time synchronization. We have time synchronization. What kind of clock? We need to use in the network. Yeah.
SPEAKER S1: Okay. Fair. I agree that that's important. Uh, a question I know that this came up, uh, at the last meeting as well. Do we have or have we, uh, added in to the requirements document, any requirements or presumptions, uh, associated with time synchronization?
SPEAKER S4: Yeah, I think that will be a good place to mention that because, you know, we in the model, we have, for example, a scheduled start time. Right. But that's based on which clock. Yeah.
SPEAKER S1: Gotcha. Okay. Thank you.
SPEAKER S3: Are you volunteering to write this document? Are you volunteering to write this document?
SPEAKER S5: Well, uh, he's just mentioned.
SPEAKER S4: In the requirement document.
SPEAKER S5: Put it.
SPEAKER S3: No, no, no, I'm talking the implementation and operational considerations document.
SPEAKER S4: That I will think about it.
SPEAKER S3: Okay.
SPEAKER S1: And I think that that's sort of a good second point to this, which is um, implementation and operational considerations presumes implementation and operations to therefore yield operational considerations. So I think something that as we look through the existing set of milestones, uh, something we're going to ask and of course we will take this to the list is, uh, should we, uh, think about removing, uh, this last milestone as a milestone for the working group, uh, and not producing an implementation and operational considerations document.
SPEAKER S4: So, um, I know people who have implemented or at least part of the data model. So we'll do some talk and come back.
SPEAKER S5: Yeah.
SPEAKER S1: Excellent. So then then my follow up ask to the working group is if, if you have a knowledge of people who have that implementation experience and interest in writing such a document down, uh, please, uh, please participate in the mailing list when we have that part of the discussion. Okay. Uh, otherwise, if we just look at where we are right now, uh, we had adopted, uh, a good set of work. Um, and we do have one, uh, personal draft related to applicability. The schedule document is going, uh, well, right now, uh, we have asked for a variety of reviews. Uh, those reviews are pending. I don't think any of those reviews have completed. Is that right, Tony? Yeah, yeah. Um, the requirements document is waiting for the shepherds write up. I will speak sternly to the shepherd momentarily, because that is me. Uh, and then, uh, we we do have the personal draft, and drafting. We'll be talking about that a little bit more today. And also the auto exposure, which I believe is also close to, um, finishing, although I will say that we did rename it to TVR off path exposure to make it not specific to Alto. So, uh, with that, uh, with, with what is a relatively short, uh, agenda for the day, we wanted to talk about and give an update on the off path mechanisms. Uh, and then, uh, some discussion around applicability and any remaining discussion around requirements, uh, to make sure that there is no additional work left. And then we would have an open mic for anyone that wants to talk about future work, uh, in TVR that we think would be important or interesting to consider in light of an adapted charter or opinions that we feel that the working group has done, uh, the work that it should be doing, and we have not been able to identify additional milestones. Right. Uh, but with that, uh, please, let's have our first presentation using off path mechanisms for exposing time variant routing information. Please. There you go. And time is okay.
SPEAKER S6: Okay. Okay. So I will be very brief. So this is just an update of the of the document. So okay basically the the well according to what was agreed in in ITA 103 in Madrid we renamed the the draft. Um, so now it's of this posture we have created uh, generated a maintenance version, uh, basically updating, uh, appendix A that was about the assessment of a particular solution of a particular solution against the TBR requirements. So basically it's a table with the requirements. We updated essentially the number of the requirements and added some additional requirements that was not covered before. And we asked about new comments, reviews, um, in the mailing list, East. But no, no feedback so far. So probably this means that the document is sufficiently stable to be progressed. Um, the regarding the next steps. So basically we'd like here to bring some point. By now, uh, we are referring to three different alternatives for of solution. They are using the auto protocol would be one. Also considering network controllers that could expose or could offer an API or an interface for retrieving the TVR information, or thinking on what is being stated in the applicability draft, either the managing device component or the database database being those components that could basically be used for off path, uh, retrieval of of the information. So the point here is that, uh, well, just to check if there is any other option, if the working group thinks and there could be any other option for playing that role of component offering the solution, and also for the cases of the network controller or the managing device to understand it would be just directly the data model. What could be the interface or the or the API would be based on this TBR model for retrieving the information. I guess that in the case of the database would not be like that. Maybe. Yes. So basically to to understand what could be the mechanism, because in the case is clear, that would be the auto protocol with the calendar functionality. But maybe for the other components, maybe we need to include some hints or what could be the way of retrieving the information. The immediate thing is the model, the the model that just to clarify that point. The second point is that we should clean up, uh, the draft by removing annexes. By now there are four annexes. Uh, my suggestion as author would be basically removing appendix B, which is about the assessment of the architecture proposal in the previous version of the applicability draft. So that draft has been expired, being substituted by the the existing one. So probably we can simply remove that also. Appendix D. That was about identifying gaps on on TBR specifications. Um this could be removed is I think is useful for basically triggering further work on Alto, but probably makes no sense to keep it here in the document. And there are two other appendices where I don't I don't have a clear position. Let's say one would be this of the assessment of Alto against TBR requirements, maybe could be basically useful for for that for getting further work on Alto. Maybe maybe we can remove. No, no I don't know. And then the appendices is about the implementation status that basically we provide some links to an open source implementation. So maybe it could be worth it to keep it just as a reference. Um, and then the point is to, to decide how to clean up. And after that, uh, basically create a new version. And if there is no there are no further comments, move the draft forward. And that's all from my side. Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: So just in light of the the previous, um, comments, if we look at appendix D, identified gaps on TVR specifications and remove but useful for further work in TVR. Uh, can you can you help me understand, uh, what is in that appendix currently? What are the identified gaps on TVR specifications?
SPEAKER S6: Uh, I will do some archaeology in my memories. For instance, uh, something that I was identifying there because in the use cases that I was thinking on was basically actions in the network. For instance, if we need to, to, uh, swap a card or whatever, if we need to identify a huge number of links that, uh, will, will be removed from the topology, uh, temporarily, we have by now as artifacts, the identification of the node and the link, but not the groupings of links. I mean, we, uh, what I was saying there is that maybe we can signal that we will remove a card, an entire card. So basically referring to more than one link or maybe, uh, these more than one could be even 72 or depending on the of the kind of router that is an example. Uh, and there will be others I cannot remember right now, but I can put in the chat now or send it to the mailing list. So basically things that could be maybe interesting to work on.
SPEAKER S1: I follow up question for the things that are identified. Would you characterize those gaps as things that are missing in existing documents, such as missing, uh, requirements, uh, or elements that would be missing in a data model? Or would they be things that are missing in terms of considerations, implementation considerations, usage considerations?
SPEAKER S6: Yes, I think so. In fact, I think that is already done in the appendix. But I if not I think I can provide my my view. Okay.
SPEAKER S1: Point being is that if, if, if we uh, upon reflection, have found additional technical materials associated to our existing documents which are coming through last calls, then, uh, identifying, uh, those, uh, sooner rather than later on the mailing list would be a priority.
SPEAKER S6: Okay. Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: All right. Um, any anything else? All right. Thank you so much.
SPEAKER S6: Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: Okay. And, uh, next up, we're going to talk about, uh, TVR applicability. And is that, uh.
SPEAKER S7: Yeah. Can you hear me?
SPEAKER S1: Yes, we can hear you.
SPEAKER S7: Uh, okay. Uh, good afternoon. And this is Linda from Huawei. I will present to the applicability of behalf of my colleagues, and I think it is very brief. Okay, so in this new version, we updated the operational considerations part based on the comments collected in the last IETF one uh and 23 meeting, and also did some editorial updates. So in detail, the first comment is, uh, it is unnecessary to require, uh, psychologization with the source of the schedule information. I think we accept this comment and updated the draft. The first change is in section 6.1. We remove the time synchronization requirements. And the second update is in section six. We added a general discussion on the, uh, that the managing devices and managed devices within a schedule domain should be synchronized with the same time zones. I think this is very reasonable. Okay. And, uh, the second comment is, you know, centralized distribution of schedule information. If a node gets a schedule that conflicts with the previous one, it may be an update of the previous mistake and should not be discarded. Uh, I think this comment is also reasonable. So we updated the section 6.4.1. We added a sentence to illustrate that if two schedules conflicts with each other, but if they have the same schedule ID, then it should be considered as an update and the latest one should be used. Uh, I think that's that's all about the updates of the new version. And we welcome for contributions and comments. Uh, and uh, it says draft ready for working group working group adoption. And I think that's all. Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: Um, so I just a few quick things before we address the the working group adoption call. Um, the first is, uh, understand, uh, and recall the discussion from 123 that, uh, we want the ability to update schedules. Um, and I just wanted to, uh, ask if there are any assumptions associated with this, uh, so that, uh, out of order delivery or, or other issues don't cause, uh, misinterpreting updates. If we're overriding schedules based on the same schedule ID, I just wanted to ask if there was any language in the document that related to the assumptions made, uh, that receiving something with the same ID would would indicate update.
SPEAKER S7: Yes, yes.
SPEAKER S1: Um, and then the, the last is, uh, the, uh, at 123, there was some discussion around whether we would add any, uh, information related to decentralized, uh, schedule generation as a, as an additional option, uh, or whether we would explicitly say that that was out of scope for, for this document. Uh, did we come to a conclusion on whether decentralized schedule generation would be a a additionally supported option?
SPEAKER S7: Uh, I think, uh, I think decentralized, uh, generation would be out of scope because, uh, uh, I didn't see the use case where the decentralized generation for schedule.
SPEAKER S1: Okay, okay. All right. Um, uh, Then the next part with chair hat on. Uh, there's a question for working group adoption. What? Uh, we will take that, of course, to the mailing list. But I wanted to pause for a moment and ask, um, if anyone has read this document and has any comments on it, uh, that we should hear prior to considering working group adoption. Um, if anyone would like to come to the mic, please do. All right. Well, thank you very much.
SPEAKER S7: Okay. So thank you.
SPEAKER S1: Okay. And then our last presentation is going to be an update on our requirements document. And this is going to be presented by I believe Brian Sipos is Brian online? Oh, there we go.
SPEAKER S8: I am yes.
SPEAKER S1: Yeah.
SPEAKER S8: So, uh, at the the earlier working group, last call, uh, there were a couple of comments and this is going to be discussing those and explaining what was done, uh, based on those comments. So if we flip to the next slide, if I can do that. Uh, so these are relatively minor changes. And that's why, uh, this is still going to be considered to be, uh, conclusions from the last call. Um, and hopefully not needing an additional last call. But the, the first comment was about, uh, the concept of multiple time variant domains in a bigger picture than just a single, uh, scheduled, uh, orchestrator. and this is now explicitly called out and explained that, um, it's possible to consider TVR in the sense of one universal domain, uh, in which everything is covered and every schedule relates to every other one logically, versus, uh, individual device domains that is more related to, uh, subdividing, uh, a large network or subdividing even pieces of a network into separate domains. And just the idea that, uh, the point of calling these out is that by dividing things into domains, that means that schedules between different domains, uh, by definition, don't relate to each other. And so this is affecting a couple different pieces of the document. Uh, but that is the, the one change and then the Next change is about, um, similar to the earlier question that was raised about time synchronization. Um, there are two aspects, and the one aspect is the notion that you have to be able to characterize your devices and the clocks on those devices to be able to know what the, what the performance of those clocks is as far as, uh, guarantees on precision, accuracy and those sort of metrics. And the second aspect is a notion that once you do have a characterization for those, the thing that generates the schedules has to be able to accommodate those using, uh, what's called in this document, uh, margins. And there's also a discussion that, uh, margin is a useful operational concept to cover both the fact that, you know, that a clock, that every single clock that executes a schedule is going to have some limited precision and some limited accuracy, and also that, you know, that real world effects of affecting a scheduled administrative change take some time to propagate into actual operational state. And so the what's discussed in these updates is this combined notion of, uh, almost an operational consideration that whoever's devising these schedules has to allow for margin for the clocks themselves and margin for execution of the schedules. And beyond this, the, uh, authors feel that these changes, um, address all of the comments received so far. And, we think that that document is ready for review.
SPEAKER S1: All right. Thank you. Brian. Uh, Rick, in the queue.
SPEAKER S2: Hey, Brian. First, an apology. I, I have not read your latest updates, but I have a very quick question on this, on this margin concept, which I think is very strong. Are you using the same value for margin to cover the drift in local clocks, and the amount of time it may take to actually, uh, react to a new schedule being published? Are you considering it to be the same value with round about the same bounds, or because I my gut tells me they could be very different?
SPEAKER S8: Sure. So because this is about sort of considerations and requirements on system design, uh, and not a specific data model, we don't we don't enforce either way, uh, whether or not a system can or must, uh, aggregate margin in the ways that you're talking about. So it's, it's just it's left open to the system design and implementation.
SPEAKER S2: Okay.
SPEAKER S8: Thank you. It's called out. It's called out as a necessary consideration. Really?
SPEAKER S1: Greg.
SPEAKER S9: Okay. Uh, Greg Erickson. So I would, uh, wanted to continue this, uh, about the timers. So is there any feeling or recommendation of, uh, what is their resolution in the clock accuracy that it will be required? Because that definitely will help to understand what mechanisms, uh, will be sufficient to guarantee such accuracy. And again, this is something that probably might be helpful to identify in the document, because that will be a guidance to their choosing, uh, solution.
SPEAKER S1: And just jump in for a moment. I think, uh, Brian, uh, disconnected and reconnected briefly during that. So if you could resume the question for him, please.
SPEAKER S9: Okay. Uh, it will be. Yeah. So the question is that any expectation of what the resolution of the accuracy in terms of what will be the units of, uh, kuac, uh.
SPEAKER S8: So similar to the. Yeah, similar to the earlier, uh, my earlier response is because this document isn't dealing with a specific system. It's it's called out that a design has to take into consideration precision and accuracy, but it's not imposing specifics at that level of detail that you're talking about, which is what are necessary units or necessary relationships for a specific system.
SPEAKER S9: Yeah, well, I think I understand that this is, uh, requirements, uh, identifying their, um, areas of, uh, attention. But giving the guidance, uh, will help to narrow or choose the reasonably sufficient solution, because, again, uh, one can say, oh, we need to synchronize it to, uh, millisecond. And the resolution of uh, 100 microseconds will be sufficient, or somebody will say, oh, we need to synchronize on a microsecond. And then, um, then the resolution of 100 nanoseconds and so on and so on. Right?
SPEAKER S8: Sure.
SPEAKER S9: Basically, is that what is good enough? Because so.
SPEAKER S8: This document, the the good thing is that in this document, it doesn't impose. The design operates in either direction, in the sense that one completely valid system can start with a clock characterization and make sure that schedules agree with that. Another completely valid system design can start with requirements on execution accuracy. And from that derive needed clock characterizations to achieve that accuracy. So this this specification calls out the relationship between them, but doesn't mandate that you have to go in one direction or the other direction in an actual system design. Okay.
SPEAKER S1: Well, just to to jump in on that, um, what we heard, what I heard was that it's not a requirement, but where would you put, uh, and document Considerations related to this and the impact of of getting that wrong. And I guess my follow up question is whether or not, uh, that kind of discussion is part of a requirements document, or whether it's part of the applicability document, or whether it would be part of some other sort of considerations document. I don't have an answer to that, by the way. I just open question. Okay.
SPEAKER S8: I don't either. Uh, but if there my only response to that is, um, if there are some specific recommendations for clarifying the document as is, that would be valuable to have as we are still going through even ISO review.
SPEAKER S1: Well, um, so so just to jump in on that real quick momentarily, Greg, if, if you were able to post that to the mailing list, uh, as a question so that it is not lost, uh, and then that would, uh, would perhaps also help us understand where best to represent the information. Because I think you're right a consideration. There needs to be a few paragraphs somewhere related to considerations like that. Yeah.
SPEAKER S6: Just to comment on top of this, I think that the accuracy will depend probably on the particular use case. I'm trying to to remind what were the use cases. For instance, there was the use case for for saving energy. Probably the accuracy there should be, um, should be let's say course compare maybe to others that imply changes in the topology of the satellites and, and the connection with the network. So, so basically probably it could be use case driven. What could be the accuracy, as Brian was commenting, is a system consideration that maybe we need to to land to the specific use case for understanding what would be the the accuracy limits that we have agreed.
SPEAKER S1: And good point. Li.
SPEAKER S7: Uh, yes, I agree with Lewis. So the accuracy of thermal segmentation is case by case. I think the different cases have different accuracy requirements. And in the applicability that we have provided several times. Several times. Psychogenic. Psychogenic mechanisms with different accuracy. And uh, I think uh, the, the the operators or who implemented the TR could select one of them based on their, uh, uh, practical requirements.
SPEAKER S1: Uh, agreed. It does seem like the applicability document would be a place to mention this. Uh, Brian with chair head on. Uh, what what I saw here is we have taken this document through working group last call, and then we did make additional changes to the document and adding subsections. Uh. The my sense is that that may, uh, cause us to want to do one more working group. Last call because of the change. Uh, do do you agree with that or does anyone have a concern with doing that? I know what you think.
SPEAKER S3: Um, I believe IETF has seen enough appeals already this year, so I would prefer to see, uh, let's go through the process and, you know, spend two weeks and do another last call.
SPEAKER S1: Uh, so with that, if if you did not have a chance to read through this document during the first working group, last call, uh, please do, uh, take a read through and we will, uh, get that last call started on the on the mailing list.
SPEAKER S8: Okay. Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: All right, so that that wraps up the discussion and the status of the work that we currently have ahead of us. Uh, what I wanted to do now was open up for open mic and simply ask, uh, do we have additional content or thoughts on additional work that should be done, uh, in this working group, either because we want to see additional items added to existing work, like applicability or thoughts on whether there is value in an additional document for implementation and operational considerations, um, or if there are other elements related to time variant routing that we think are of interest and for which there is energy to do work, uh, in this working group. So we'll take a few minutes now and open up the mic. And I will also say we are all aware in time variant routing that sometimes a link leaves early. Uh, and we can also wrap up a few minutes early, uh, as well. But let's give it a minute now to see if anyone would like to join the queue. All right. So what I will say is, uh, we will yield back, uh, 27 minutes of time. Uh, I'd like to say thank you. Everyone who has participated, particularly on the mailing list and in the discussions and in the document reviews. Uh, we will likely then, uh, put a call together for the requirements document. We will see if there's any action needed on the data models as they come through the various reviews. Uh, and then we will put out the working group adoption call for the applicability document. And please do chime in on the mailing list to see whether there is additional need and energy for an implementation and operational considerations document separate from the applicability document. Uh, but otherwise, uh, we will, uh, look forward to seeing everyone here at 125. Thank you so much. Yeah, we can clap. It's okay. Okay. All right. And thank you all who are online. You get one clap online.
SPEAKER S3: Something about.
SPEAKER S1: Oh, sorry, I think I unplugged you on. No, I definitely unplugged, you know, more energy for you.
SPEAKER S10: Yeah. That's fine. All right. Sounds good. It was so short. It was the glory. The glory. It was time invariant. So I sent you a link.

SPEAKER S8: So, uh, at the the earlier working group, last call, uh, there were a couple of comments and this is going to be discussing those and explaining what was done, uh, based on those comments.

Uh, so these are relatively minor changes.
And that's why, uh, this is still going to be considered to be, uh, conclusions from the last call.
Um, and hopefully not needing an additional last call.
But the, the first comment was about, uh, the concept of multiple time variant domains in a bigger picture than just a single, uh, scheduled, uh, orchestrator.

and this is now explicitly called out and explained that, um, it's possible to consider TVR in the sense of one universal domain, uh, in which everything is covered and every schedule relates to every other one logically, versus, uh, individual device domains that is more related to, uh, subdividing, uh, a large network or subdividing even pieces of a network into separate domains.
And just the idea that, uh, the point of calling these out is that by dividing things into domains, that means that schedules between different domains, uh, by definition, don't relate to each other.
And so this is affecting a couple different pieces of the document.
Uh, but that is the, the one change and then the Next change is about, um, similar to the earlier question that was raised about time synchronization.

Um, there are two aspects, and the one aspect is the notion that you have to be able to characterize your devices and the clocks on those devices to be able to know what the, what the performance of those clocks is as far as, uh, guarantees on precision, accuracy and those sort of metrics.

And the second aspect is a notion that once you do have a characterization for those, the thing that generates the schedules has to be able to accommodate those using, uh, what's called in this document, uh, margins.

And there's also a discussion that, uh, margin is a useful operational concept to cover both the fact that, you know, that a clock, that every single clock that executes a schedule is going to have some limited precision and some limited accuracy, and also that, you know, that real world effects of affecting a scheduled administrative change take some time to propagate into actual operational state.

And so the what's discussed in these updates is this combined notion of, uh, almost an operational consideration that whoever's devising these schedules has to allow for margin for the clocks themselves and margin for execution of the schedules.
And beyond this, the, uh, authors feel that these changes, um, address all of the comments received so far.
And, we think that that document is ready for review.
